horizonPanel.title: Horizon Panel
horizonPanel.intro: The primary guidance HUD — use it as a combined stack or break it into three independently positioned panels.
horizonPanel.overviewBody1: The Horizon Panel occupies the full available vertical space on the guidance home screen. It is only visible in the guidance navigation state — in free driving, the Upcoming Events Panel appears separately.
horizonPanel.overviewBody2: In its default composed state, the three sub-components share interaction contracts: tapping the ETA area opens route options, and the panel resizes cohesively when the safe area changes. When decomposed, each sub-component is positioned and themed independently.
horizonPanel.decomposeBody: Choose whether to use the Horizon Panel as a single composed block, or break it apart into independent sub-components (NIP, Upcoming Events, ETA Panel) that can each be placed anywhere on the guidance screen.
horizonPanel.decomposeCallout: When decomposed, sub-component interaction contracts become optional. For example, tapping the ETA Panel to open route options can be disabled when it is positioned far from the NIP.
horizonPanel.positionBody: In composed mode, place the Horizon Panel on the left, center, or right side of the guidance screen. The map safe area adjusts automatically.

theme.notes: Colors, typography, icon assets, spacing, radius, border, docked/floating — carry-over from ANA

decompose.req: Break apart into NIP + Upcoming Events + ETA Panel
decompose.notes: Very popular market request; core of VW group UX vision

reposition.notes: Not needed for R2 or VW PoC

navControls.title: Navigation Controls
navControls.intro: Position the navigation button bar on any screen edge and choose which actions — search, charging, mute, and settings — are visible.
navControls.overviewBody1: The Map Controls Button Bar surfaces primary navigation actions on the home screen. It is positioned by the integrator as a vertical or horizontal strip on any edge of the navigation application area. The bar is owned by UX Library — TomTom controls interaction quality and accessibility while OEMs control placement, content, and visual theming.
navControls.callout: Button bar configuration was previously documented in Home Screen Layout. It has been consolidated here to keep all navigation controls customisation in one place.
navControls.positionBody: Place the bar on any edge of the screen and control which buttons are shown. The map safe area and other UI elements adjust automatically.
navControls.searchBody: By default, search is surfaced as a persistent destination entry panel on the map. Alternatively, collapse it to a compact search button inside the controls bar to free up map space for a cleaner idle state.

theme.req: Theme the Map Controls button bar
theme.notes: Colors, typography, icon assets, spacing, radius, border — carry-over from ANA

toggle.notes: Part of VW group UX vision

searchBtn.req: Show search as button entry point in bar
searchBtn.notes: Hides destination entry panel; part of VW group UX vision

both.req: Button bar on both sides simultaneously

custom.req: Add custom button to button bar

homeScreen.title: Home Screen Layout
homeScreen.intro: Configure how navigation shares the IVI home screen — zone sizing, widget strip placement, and state signals for the orchestrator.
homeScreen.overviewBody1: Embedding the map as the IVI home screen wallpaper keeps spatial awareness continuously visible and reduces the interaction depth required to start navigation. The orchestrator retains full control of layout: it decides the size of the navigation application area and when to show or collapse domain widgets, based on the state signals the navigation app exposes.
homeScreen.callout1: Navigation in a dedicated window or view is a separate integration pattern covered in a different PRD. This page covers the map as wallpaper pattern only.
homeScreen.zonesIntro: The IVI interface is divided into named areas. Click a zone below to see where it sits on screen and what it contains.

nav.label: Navigation Application Area
nav.desc: The area where all navigation map content and features reside. The IVI orchestrator controls its size.

safe.label: Map Safe Area
safe.desc: The portion of the screen where the map renders critical content without obstruction. Contains the recenter button and compass.

display.label: Map Display Area
display.desc: The full map area. Can be covered by navigation elements or IVI widgets such as media panels.

controls.label: Map Controls Zone
controls.desc: Primary controls surfaced on the navigation home screen — Search, Charging Finder, Mute, Quick Settings.

homeScreen.resizeBody: The orchestrator can dynamically resize the navigation application area from any side — top, bottom, left, or right — simultaneously. The navigation app transitions smoothly to the new area with a configurable animation. Drag the sliders below to see how the application area and generated code change.
homeScreen.callout2: Resizing can occur on multiple sides simultaneously. The navigation app must adhere to the viewable application area across all flows and screens.
homeScreen.statesIntro: Rather than a binary active/passive flag, the navigation app exposes four orthogonal observable state dimensions. Use the selector below to explore each state combination.
homeScreen.callout3: Button bar position, visibility, and search entry point configuration has moved to Navigation Controls in the App Customisation section.

activity.label: Navigation Activity State
activity.desc: High-level navigation activity regardless of UI interaction.

panel.label: UI Panel State
panel.desc: Which navigation UI panel, if any, is currently foregrounded. NONE = default map view.

map.label: Map Interaction State
map.desc: Whether the map is auto-following the vehicle or being actively panned/zoomed.

trip.label: Trip Planning State
trip.desc: Lifecycle of the current trip planning session.

homeScreen.passiveDesc: Orchestrator may show domain widgets over the map.
homeScreen.activeDesc: Orchestrator should hide or collapse domain widgets.

area.notes: All flows and screens must adhere to the configured area

resize.notes: Top, bottom, left, right — simultaneously, with animation

state.notes: Activity, Panel, Map Interaction, Trip Planning

anim.notes: Orchestrator-controlled so widget animations can be synchronised

safe.notes: Resize horizon panel when safe area is compromised

display.notes: Center, passenger, and rear seat displays

searchEngine.title: Search engine
searchEngine.intro: Plug in any search provider behind UX Library's panel — 3rd-party online results, automatic offline fallback to TomTom onboard search.
searchEngine.overviewBody: The STLA R2 programme requires Stellantis to use Google Search when the vehicle is online while preserving TomTom onboard search for offline scenarios. UX Library achieves this by abstracting the search provider behind a configurable interface.
searchEngine.callout1: The offline provider is always required — even when an online provider is configured. UX Library automatically routes queries to the offline provider when connectivity is unavailable.
searchEngine.callout2: "Powered by Google" attribution is a legal commitment for STLA R2. It must appear in both the search bar and results list whenever the 3rd-party provider is active.
searchEngine.callout3: When connectivity drops while viewing an LPP, the cached version is shown and Drive / Route actions continue to work.
searchEngine.callout4: When the map shows a mix of TomTom and STLA POIs, configure the source routing so each POI type calls the right API.

designTokens.title: Design tokens
designTokens.quickAnswer: Named design variables for colours, type, spacing, and more — expressed as platform-agnostic values that resolve at build time.
designTokens.introBody3: If TomTom updates its brand colour, every component updates automatically — you change the token value once.
designTokens.structureBody: TomTom uses a three-tier token architecture. Each tier builds on the one below it.
designTokens.globalTokensDesc: Raw values — hex codes, pixel sizes, font names. Never reference these in components directly. Example: tt_glb_color_red_300 = #C82020.
designTokens.systemTokensDesc: System tokens give semantic meaning to global values. tt_sys_color_brand_primary doesn't say "blue" — it says "primary brand colour". This level is where themes are swapped; changing a system token value propagates to all components that reference it.
designTokens.componentTokensDesc: Component tokens apply system tokens to specific UI element properties. They allow fine-grained control of individual components without breaking the overall token structure. A component token always resolves through the system layer to a global value at runtime.
designTokens.overviewBody: Only Prefix, Tier, and Type are required — the remaining parts are used when the token needs to express more specificity.
designTokens.part0Desc: Identifies which organisation the token belongs to. tt is always the TomTom prefix.
designTokens.part1Desc: Shows which level the token belongs to: glb (global), sys (system), cmp (component).
designTokens.part2Desc: Specifies the design property: color, font, size, spacing, radius, opacity.
designTokens.part3Desc: Further qualifies the type: background, foreground, border, surface, etc.
designTokens.part4Desc: Names the logical role: primary, secondary, brand, guidance, alert, etc.
designTokens.part5Desc: Identifies sub-elements within a larger component, e.g. a label inside a button chip: chip_label.
designTokens.part6Desc: Interaction state of the element: default, focused, pressed, disabled, selected.
designTokens.examplesBody: Tokens span all visual properties. Below are representative examples of each tier applied to colour.
designTokens.sys0Desc: Main navigation canvas — resolves to blue_700
designTokens.sys1Desc: TomTom brand red — resolves to red_300
designTokens.sys3Desc: Route polyline on map — resolves to green_300
designTokens.namingTitle: TomTom token logic and naming principles
designTokens.namingBody: TomTom tokens follow internally agreed naming standards at the token type level.
designTokens.naming0Notes: Maps to a typeface name (e.g. Noto Sans)
designTokens.naming3Notes: Space between related elements — always a multiple of 4
designTokens.naming4Notes: Applies to prominent, large-format text. Sizes: s · m · l · xl · xxl
designTokens.calloutItem0b: prefixed token — never hardcode raw values
designTokens.calloutItem1a: System tokens are designed to support both
designTokens.calloutItem2a: Token names use camelCase for multi-word segments:
designTokens.themesBody1: Using design tokens, you can apply multiple modes to all relevant design properties — colour, typography, and sizing — simultaneously, without touching component code. There are three distinct mode types:
designTokens.themesCallout: Never hardcode hex values in component code. If a value doesn't have a token, request one from the design system team — do not work around the system with inline values.
designTokens.benefit0Desc: Swap entire colour modes (light/dark/OEM brand) by changing token values, not component code.
designTokens.benefit1Desc: Every component that references the same token automatically stays in sync.
designTokens.benefit2Desc: Build new screens faster — pull from a shared, pre-approved token set instead of designing from scratch.
designTokens.benefit3Desc: Tokens enforce constraints. If a value isn't in the system, it needs a design decision — not a one-off hex.

cluster.title: Displaying on Cluster
cluster.intro: Mirror navigation guidance — manoeuvres, speed limit, lane guidance, and ETA — onto the vehicle's instrument cluster.
cluster.apiDesc: Start and configure the ClusterActivity to render navigation info on vehicle instrument cluster displays.
cluster.overviewBody: ClusterActivity is started from the host application using an explicit Android Intent with a fixed action and category. The initial configuration — which components to show, their layout types, and their pixel dimensions — is passed as a JSON string in the intent extra. Any parameter can be updated at runtime by re-sending the same intent with only the changed fields.
cluster.overviewCallout: ClusterActivity handles navigation-state transitions automatically: it shows or hides components based on whether guidance is active, without any additional calls from the host app.
cluster.startBody: The activity must be started with a specific action and NAVIGATION category. The full initial configuration is passed as a JSON string in the clusterConfigurationUpdate extra.
cluster.configBody: Adjust the controls below to explore how each parameter affects the cluster display. Components stack top-to-bottom in the navigation column: NIP → SLG → CMP → JV/UEP → ETA.

SLG.label: Simple Lane Guidance
SLG.desc: Row of lane arrows beneath the instruction

UEP.label: Upcoming Events
UEP.desc: Cameras, hazards and lane closures ahead

ETA.label: ETA
ETA.desc: Arrival time strip at the bottom

cluster.positionBody: The Horizon Panel and safe area can be given precise pixel bounds using hpComponentRect and safeAreaComponentRect. Set displayPX: true when your cluster display uses raw pixel values rather than density-independent units.
cluster.runtimeBody: Partial updates are supported — only the fields included in the JSON will change. Useful for day/night style switching or toggling guidance elements when navigation state changes.

positionRect.req: Position HP and safe area via pixel rect

runtimeUpdate.notes: Re-send intent with only changed keys

displayPx.notes: displayPX — use true for fixed-res clusters

routingIntro.title: Routing API
routingIntro.quickAnswer: Calculate optimal routes between waypoints with full support for vehicle profiles, real-time traffic, EV consumption modelling, reachable range, and batch processing across ~215 countries.

vehicleProfiles.title: Vehicle profiles
vehicleProfiles.desc: Truck routing with height, width, weight, axle load, ADR tunnel codes, and hazardous load types. Eight travel modes.

evConsumption.title: EV consumption model
evConsumption.desc: Speed-to-kWh consumption tables, auxiliary power draw, and regenerative braking — for single-charge EV routes.

traffic.title: Live + historic traffic
traffic.desc: IQ Routes™ learns from historical patterns. Real-time incident data projects travel time up to 60 days forward.

guidance.title: Turn-by-turn guidance
guidance.desc: 40+ manoeuvre codes, lane guidance with separator types, road shield references, and phonetic street names.

routingIntro.endpointsSubtitle: Three core endpoints plus two guidance surfaces. Each can be used independently.
routingIntro.demosIntro: Live demos built with the TomTom Maps SDK for JavaScript on top of the Routing API. Click Key config to see the API parameters in action.
routingIntro.readyBody: The Quick Start walks through authentication and your first route request in under 5 minutes.
routingIntro.relatedBody: These APIs are separate products — each with their own endpoint, quota, and documentation.

relatedLdevr.label: Long Distance EV Routing API
relatedLdevr.desc: Automatic charging stop selection for trips beyond a single charge. Separate product with its own quota and tier.

relatedMatrix.label: Matrix Routing v2 API
relatedMatrix.desc: Travel time and distance for every origin–destination combination. Up to 100M cells asynchronously.

relatedWaypoint.label: Waypoint Optimization API
relatedWaypoint.desc: Reorder 2–12 stops for minimum travel time. Feed the result back into Calculate Route.

routingIntro.callout: Coverage data, changelog, and deprecation notices are in Market Coverage and the Platform Reference section.

endpointDescs.calculateRoute: Calculate one or more routes between waypoints with full vehicle profile, traffic, consumption model, and guidance support.
endpointDescs.reachableRange: Calculate the isochrone polygon reachable within a given time, distance, fuel, or energy budget from an origin point.
endpointDescs.batchRouting: Submit up to 700 route or range calculations in a single request. Synchronous (100 items) and asynchronous modes.
endpointDescs.instructions: Activate maneuver-by-maneuver guidance on any Calculate Route call. Returns text, tagged, or coded maneuver arrays.
endpointDescs.laneGuidance: Per-lane direction and drivability data at complex junctions. Requires instructionsType=tagged and sectionType=lanes.
endpointDescs.roadShields: Shield shape category, road reference, and display text for every road segment. Use iconCategory to select the correct sign asset.

exampleDescs.reachableRanges: Calculate time-based accessibility zones from one or more origins and render them as colour-filled polygons on a live map.
exampleDescs.routeWithWaypoints: Calculate and render an A–B route on an interactive map. Add intermediate waypoints, choose travel mode, and display route summary stats.

ldevrIntro.title: Long Distance EV Routing API
ldevrIntro.quickAnswer: Plan routes for electric vehicles over distances that exceed a single charge. The API automatically selects optimal charging stops — factoring in connector type, charger power, detour cost, and battery state — and returns a complete itinerary with dwell times and station details ready to render in-app.

batteryModelling.title: Battery state modelling
batteryModelling.desc: Simulates charge level along every road segment — accounting for speed, elevation, auxiliary power draw, and regenerative braking.

stopSelection.title: Automatic stop selection
stopSelection.desc: Picks the combination of charging stops that minimises total journey time, including dwell at each charger.

connectorMatching.title: Connector matching
connectorMatching.desc: Pass the connectors your vehicle supports (CCS, CHAdeMO, GB/T…) and only compatible stations are considered.

trafficEnergy.title: Traffic-aware energy
trafficEnergy.desc: Real-time and historic traffic affects modelled consumption — stop-and-go driving uses more energy than free-flow.

ldevrIntro.endpointsSubtitle: Two endpoints. Use only what you need — each can be called standalone.

endpointDescs.calculateEvRoute: Plan a multi-leg EV route with automatic charging stop selection. Returns a complete itinerary with station names, connector details, dwell times, and battery state at every leg boundary.
endpointDescs.batchEvRoute: Submit up to 700 EV route calculations in a single call. Synchronous (100 items) and asynchronous modes. Each item carries its own vehicle and battery parameters.

ldevrIntro.demosIntro: Live demos built with the TomTom Maps SDK for JavaScript on top of the LDEVR API. Click Key config to see the API parameters in action.

exampleDescs.ldevrModelId: A–B Long Distance EV Route from Paris to Amsterdam with automatic charging stops, using a registered vehicle model variant.
exampleDescs.customChargingStops: Munich to Paris EV route with charging stops rendered using custom SVG icons mapped by charger speed — slow, regular and fast.
exampleDescs.ldevrDetailedVehicle: Paris to Amsterdam EV route with a fully specified vehicle — battery capacity, connector types, consumption curves and auxiliary power load.

baseUrlRows.authDesc: API key via ?key= query parameter or Authorization: apiKey header
baseUrlRows.methodDesc: POST — route points in the URL path; all vehicle and EV parameters in the JSON request body
baseUrlRows.planTierDesc: Developer, Flex, Enterprise — not included in the free Routing API quota

whenUseItems: The trip exceeds the vehicle's single-charge range
You want the API to select charging stops automatically
You need station brand, power, and connector data in the response
You are building an IVI or companion app with charging UX
You use the TomTom Navigation SDK and want charging stop cards
whenNotItems: The trip fits within a single charge
You only need to model battery consumption, not plan stops
You are routing a combustion or hybrid vehicle
You need Orbis Maps v2 compatibility today
You want alternatives, truck routing, or bicycle/pedestrian modes
ldevrIntro.readyBody: The Quick Start walks through authentication and your first EV route request — with a working cURL example and Kotlin SDK snippet — in under 5 minutes.

anaIntro.title: Automotive Navigation Application
anaIntro.quickAnswer: The Automotive Navigation Application (ANA) is TomTom's pre-built navigation APK for Android Automotive OS. OEMs integrate it as a complete navigation solution and connect to it through two lightweight layers — VIL and CIL — rather than building navigation from scratch.
anaIntro.heroAlt: ANA — live traffic and better route suggestion on AAOS

fastTimeToMarket.title: Fast time to market
fastTimeToMarket.desc: No navigation UI to build or test — integration is measured in weeks, not months.

tomtomMaintained.title: TomTom-maintained UX
tomtomMaintained.desc: Map content, routing algorithms, and UI improvements ship as APK updates with no OEM effort.

fullEvSupport.title: Full EV support
fullEvSupport.desc: Battery-integrated route planning, connector-aware search, and SoC display are included out of the box.

aaOsCertified.title: AAOS-certified
aaOsCertified.desc: ANA is designed and tested for Android Automotive OS and passes AAOS compatibility requirements.

limitedCustomisation.title: Limited UI customisation
limitedCustomisation.descPart1: ANA supports token-based theming but the navigation UI layout is fixed. For more UI flexibility, see
limitedCustomisation.descPart2: for customisable pre-built components, or the

apkDependency.title: APK dependency
apkDependency.desc: Your IVI integrates ANA as a separate APK via two lightweight APIs — VIL feeds vehicle data in, CIL lets other vehicle software control the app. You do not own the navigation source code.

anaIntro.screenshotsSubtitle: ANA delivers a complete AAOS navigation experience out of the box — from premium map rendering to automated EV route planning.

liveTraffic.label: Live Traffic & Smart Rerouting
liveTraffic.desc: Real-time traffic overlays with automatic better-route suggestions during active navigation.

premiumMap.label: Premium Map Display
premiumMap.desc: High-fidelity 3D vector maps with day and night modes, fully styled to OEM brand guidelines.

evCharging.label: EV Charging Station Search
evCharging.desc: Connector-aware search shows nearby stations with live availability and power ratings.

ldEvRoute.label: Long-Distance EV Route Planning
ldEvRoute.desc: Automatic charging stop insertion based on battery state and connector compatibility.

anaIntro.featuresSubtitle: ANA ships with a complete set of navigation features. OEMs configure rather than build.

driverExperience.title: Driver Experience
driverExperience.desc: Full turn-by-turn navigation UI — search, route planning, guidance, ETA panel, traffic alerts, and arrival — all pre-built and tested.

vil.title: Vehicle Integration Layer
vil.desc: Feed vehicle signals into ANA: EV battery state, connector type, GDPR consent, and cluster output. VIL is your data pipe into the navigation engine.

cil.title: Car Integration Layer
cil.desc: Command ANA from your app: trigger navigation to a destination, cancel routes, search nearby POIs, and query live navigation state.

theming.title: OEM Theming
theming.desc: Override design tokens — colours, typography, corner radius, and icon sets — to match your brand without touching ANA internals.

evSupport.title: EV Support
evSupport.desc: Connector-aware charging station search, battery-integrated long-range routing, and real-time SoC display built in from day one.

anaIntro.archBody: ANA sits between your vehicle software and the TomTom platform. VIL flows data up; CIL sends commands down. Your HMI app never calls TomTom APIs directly.

archLayers.vilDesc: Push vehicle data into ANA. Feed EV state, GDPR consent, speed, and cluster data to the navigation engine.
archLayers.cilDesc: Control ANA from your app. Trigger navigation, set destinations, manage routes, and query nav state.
archLayers.anaDesc: TomTom-maintained navigation APK. Full AAOS navigation experience: map, search, routing, EV charging, guidance, cluster output, and OEM theming — all pre-built and tested.

anaIntro.readyBody: Start with the System Overview to understand how ANA fits into your IVI stack, then follow the Quick Start guide to get navigation running in your first session.
anaIntro.callout: ANA is targeted at Android Automotive OS. It is not a general Android app and does not run on standard Android phones or tablets. For phone or tablet navigation, use the Maps & Navigation SDK.

navsdkIntro.title: Maps & Navigation SDK
navsdkIntro.quickAnswerAndroid: Build a fully custom Android navigation experience from the ground up. The SDK gives you independent, composable layers — vector map rendering, multi-waypoint routing with EV and traffic support, full turn-by-turn guidance, ADAS-quality virtual horizon data, and offline maps — so you use only what you need and own every detail of the UI.
navsdkIntro.quickAnswerIos: Build a fully custom iOS navigation experience from the ground up. The SDK gives you independent, composable layers — vector map rendering, multi-waypoint routing with EV and traffic support, full turn-by-turn guidance, ADAS-quality virtual horizon data, and native CarPlay integration — so you use only what you need and own every detail of the UI.
navsdkIntro.whenTitle: When to choose the Maps & Navigation SDK

fullUiOwnership.title: Full UI ownership
fullUiOwnership.desc: Design and maintain your own navigation experience end to end. No TomTom UI patterns to inherit — your designers own every pixel.

modularByDesign.title: Modular by design
modularByDesign.desc: Use only the layers you need: just map rendering, just routing, or the full guidance stack. Pay no overhead for features you don't ship.

androidAndIos.title: Android & iOS
androidAndIos.desc: One SDK family covers both platforms — Compose and Views on Android, SwiftUI and UIKit on iOS — with a consistent API surface.

extensible.title: Extensible
extensible.desc: Plug in your own search provider, custom route post-processing, third-party voice engine, or ADAS data feed alongside the SDK.

youBuildUiAndroid.title: You build the UI
youBuildUiAndroid.descPart1: Significant development investment. For ready-made navigation components on Android,
youBuildUiAndroid.descPart2: gives you a full Compose UI stack built on top of this SDK.

youBuildUiIos.title: You build the UI
youBuildUiIos.desc: Significant development investment. There is no pre-built UI library for iOS — your team designs and builds all navigation UI on top of the SDK primitives.

longerTimeAndroid.title: Longer time to market
longerTimeAndroid.descPart1: Expect months of development for a production-grade experience. For faster delivery on Android, start with
longerTimeAndroid.descPart2: . For a fully pre-built solution, consider

longerTimeIos.title: Longer time to market
longerTimeIos.descPart1: Expect months of development for a production-grade iOS experience. For a fully pre-built automotive navigation solution, consider

navsdkIntro.capabilitiesSubtitleAndroid: Each capability is independently usable. Use only the map, or combine all layers to build a complete Android navigation application.
navsdkIntro.capabilitiesSubtitleIos: Each capability is independently usable. Use only the map, or combine all layers to build a complete iOS navigation application.

mapDisplay.title: Map Display
mapDisplay.desc: Render TomTom vector maps with full control over style, camera, layers, markers, and traffic overlays.

search.title: Search
search.desc: Plug in address search, fuzzy POI search, and EV charging search through a unified search interface.

routing.title: Routing
routing.desc: Calculate multi-waypoint routes with vehicle profiles, traffic, EV range, and alternative route support.

navigation.title: Navigation
navigation.desc: Full turn-by-turn guidance with voice announcements, lane guidance, and real-time re-routing.

virtualHorizon.title: Virtual Horizon
virtualHorizon.desc: Access ADAS-quality road attribute data ahead of the vehicle for speed limit, curve, and hazard warnings.

offlineMaps.title: Offline Maps
offlineMaps.desc: Download regional map packages for full offline navigation without a data connection.

carPlay.title: CarPlay
carPlay.desc: Integrate with Apple CarPlay using a navigation session API built for the CarPlay framework.

navsdkIntro.archBody: One dependency, the full navigation stack. Your app sits on top of the SDK — TomTom APIs and map data are bundled and fully abstracted away.

tomtomPlatform.label: TomTom Platform
tomtomPlatform.desc: Maps · Traffic · POI · EV Data · ADAS

compose: First-class Compose API for MapDisplay and all UI components
views: Full Views-based API for existing codebases and AAOS systems

swiftui: Native SwiftUI MapView and composable navigation components

navsdkIntro.readyBodyAndroid: The quickest path to a working map is the Getting Started guide — it walks through project setup, SDK initialisation, and displaying your first map in under 15 minutes.
navsdkIntro.readyBodyIos: Follow the iOS Getting Started guide to configure your Xcode project, add the Swift Package, and display your first map.
navsdkIntro.callout: This documentation covers SDK v2. If you are migrating from SDK v1, see the Migration Guide in Getting Started.
